title: [Metal][cache] dual-hash verification for MPSGraphCache and MPSKernelCache (#77176)

labels.name: release notes: mps
labels.description: Release notes category

labels.name: ciflow/mps
labels.description: Run MPS tests (subset of trunk)

body:
## Summary

Adds dual-hash verification to `MPSGraphCache` and `MPSKernelCache`. The single `std::hash<std::string>` cache key is supplemented with an independent FNV-1a secondary hash, computed once at insertion and verified on every cache hit. Catches primary-hash collisions at runtime instead of silently returning the wrong cached graph or kernel in release builds (the existing `TORCH_INTERNAL_ASSERT_DEBUG_ONLY` only fires in debug builds).

A correct, non-colliding workload sees no behavioral change. On a primary-hash collision: lookup treats the entry as a miss (recompiles); insertion into an occupied slot is a hard `TORCH_CHECK` error. Probability of both hashes colliding simultaneously: ~1 / 2^128.

## Test

```
$ python test/test_mps.py -v -k TestCacheIntegrity
```

Tests live in `test/test_mps.py::TestCacheIntegrity` and cover repeated-op consistency, distinct-op non-contamination across both unary and binary, dtype independence, matmul cache correctness, and cache hit consistency after many entries.

Fixes #77176.

title: Fix torch.special.zeta returning NaN for q=+inf
body: zeta(s, +inf) returned NaN instead of 0 for s > 1. The tail expansion computes b * w where b = 0 and w = inf, producing NaN. Added isinf(q) early return in both CPU (Math.h) and CUDA jiterator (Math.cuh) paths. Added test_zeta_with_inf_q covering all-inf and mixed finite/inf inputs. Fixes #187294

title: [Metal][memory] add configurable memory budget API for MPS allocator (#105839)

labels.name: release notes: mps
labels.description: Release notes category

labels.name: ciflow/mps
labels.description: Run MPS tests (subset of trunk)

body:
## Summary

Adds `torch.mps.set_memory_budget(bytes)` / `torch.mps.get_memory_budget()` for the MPS allocator. When set, the allocator caps total GPU memory and evicts cached blocks before raising a clean OOM. Default `0` keeps current behavior (unlimited).

On Apple Silicon, GPU and CPU share unified memory, so an unconstrained MPS workload can consume most of system RAM. This PR adds a per-process MPS-only cap.

## Usage

```python
import torch
torch.mps.set_memory_budget(2 * 1024**3)   # 2 GiB cap
torch.mps.get_memory_budget()               # 2147483648
torch.mps.set_memory_budget(0)              # disable the cap
```

## Test

```
$ python test/test_mps.py -v -k TestMemoryBudget
```

Tests live in `test/test_mps.py::TestMemoryBudget` and cover set/get round-trip, type validation, multiple-set last-wins, ops correctness with budget set, and ops correctness after budget reset.

Fixes #105839, #132086.

title: Fix Tensor.to() ignoring memory_format for non-contiguous tensors
body:
**Root cause:** `to_will_alias()` used `suggest_memory_format()` which returns `Contiguous` as a default for anything not channels-last shaped — incorrectly skipping the copy for stride-0 (`expand`) and permuted-stride
(`transpose`) tensors.
**Fix:** Replace with `is_contiguous(memory_format)` which reads the actual `TensorImpl` contiguity flags.

```cpp
// Before
self.suggest_memory_format() == memory_format
// After
self.is_contiguous(memory_format)
Already-contiguous tensors are unaffected — no performance regression.
```

Co-authored-by: Claude 

Fixes: #132020, 
Fixes: #62027, 
Fixes: #86558

title: [inductor] add alpha != 0 check to celu decomposition to match eager validation
body:
## Description

`torch.compile` with the `inductor` backend silently produces `NaN` when `torch.celu_()` or `torch.celu()` is called with `alpha=0.0`. Eager mode correctly raises a `RuntimeError`.

**Reproducer:**
```python
import torch

def f(x, alpha):
    return torch.celu_(x, alpha)

x = torch.tensor([[-2.0, -0.5, 0.0], [1.0, 3.0, -4.0]])

# Eager: RuntimeError: ZeroDivisionError: alpha cannot be 0 for CELU
try:
    f(x.clone(), 0.0)
except RuntimeError as e:
    print("eager:", e)

# Compiled (before fix): silently returns NaN
out = torch.compile(f, backend="inductor")(x.clone(), 0.0)
print("compiled:", out)  # tensor([[-0., -0., nan], [1., 3., -0.]])
```

## Root Cause

The `celu` decomposition in `torch/_refs/nn/functional/__init__.py` performs `torch.true_divide(a, alpha)` without first validating `alpha`. When `alpha=0`, this produces `inf`/`nan` instead of raising. Because the C++ kernel path is bypassed under `torch.compile`, the eager-mode validation never runs.

## Fix

Add `torch._check(alpha != 0, ...)` before the division, following the exact pattern already used in `exponential_`, `cauchy_`, `log_normal_`, and `softshrink` decompositions:

```python
torch._check(
    alpha != 0,
    lambda: "ZeroDivisionError: alpha cannot be 0 for CELU",
)
```

Because `alpha` is always a Python scalar (not a tensor), the check evaluates correctly during both eager execution and inductor's FakeTensor tracing phase — no graph break.

## Testing

Added `test_celu_zero_alpha_raises` to `test/inductor/test_cpu_repro.py` which verifies both eager and compiled paths raise `RuntimeError` with `alpha=0`, and that valid `alpha` values continue to work.

Fixes #183762

body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

I work on xFormers and last year we made xFormers switch to the PyTorch stable API/ABI, so that our pre-built wheels would be forward-compatible with later PyTorch releases. This worked well, but this contract got "broken" when PyTorch switched its default CUDA version from 12.x to 13.x for the wheels pushed to pypi.org.

Concretely, this means that xFormers cannot push a single wheel to PyPI that will work across all PyTorch versions 2.10+ (which is what we're aiming for) because if we push a CUDA 12.x wheel we'll become incompatible with PT 2.12+ and if we push a CUDA 13.x wheel we become incompatible with PT 2.10-11.

To be clear, any installation happening from PyTorch's own indices (`https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cuXXY`) still works fine.

Is there a plan to make this work?

We could consider statically linking to the CUDA runtime within xFormers, but that seems a rather extreme solution.

title: torch.compile FX graph does not distinguish custom ops with the same name but different mutates_args

labels.name: module: custom-operators
labels.description: custom operators, custom ops, custom-operators, custom-ops

labels.name: module: correctness (silent)
labels.description: issue that returns an incorrect result silently

labels.name: module: pt2-dispatcher
labels.description: PT2  dispatcher-related issues (e.g., aotdispatch, functionalization, faketensor, custom-op,

labels.name: bot-triaged
labels.description: This is a label only to be used by the auto triage bot

body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

In `torch.compile`, changing only `mutates_args` of a custom op does not change its FX graph. As a result, custom ops with the same name but different `mutates_args` can hit the same Inductor cache entry.

This can cause incorrect behavior. For example, as shown in the code below, for a custom op that returns `None`, Inductor may eliminate the op when `mutates_args` is empty, but preserve it when `mutates_args` is non-empty. Since both versions share the same FX graph and cache key, a stale cached result can still be reused after `mutates_args` is corrected, unless the cache is manually cleared.


```python
# Test test_fx_graph_cache.py
# REMOVE_OLD_CACHE=1 python test_fx_graph_cache.py
# ADD_MUTATES_ARGS=1 python test_fx_graph_cache.py
# REMOVE_OLD_CACHE=1 ADD_MUTATES_ARGS=1 python test_fx_graph_cache.py

import os
import shutil
if os.environ.get("REMOVE_OLD_CACHE", "0") == "1":
    shutil.rmtree(f"/tmp/torchinductor_{os.environ.get('USER')}", ignore_errors=True)

import torch
from torch.library import Library, infer_schema  # noqa
from typing import Callable

test_lib = Library("test", "FRAGMENT")  # noqa

def direct_register_custom_op(
    op_name: str,
    op_func: Callable,
    mutates_args: list[str] | None = None,
    fake_impl: Callable | None = None,
    target_lib: Library | None = None,
    dispatch_key: str | None = None,
    tags: tuple[torch.Tag, ...] = (),
    schema_str: str | None = None,
):
    if mutates_args is None:
        mutates_args = []

    if dispatch_key is None:
        dispatch_key = "CUDA"

    if schema_str is None:
        schema_str = infer_schema(op_func, mutates_args=mutates_args)
    # print(f"Registering custom op '{op_name}' with schema: {schema_str} to library: {target_lib or test_lib}")

    my_lib = target_lib or test_lib
    my_lib.define(op_name + schema_str, tags=tags)
    my_lib.impl(op_name, op_func, dispatch_key=dispatch_key)
    if fake_impl is not None:
        my_lib._register_fake(op_name, fake_impl)


def create_output(shape, dtype, device):
    return torch.empty(shape, dtype=dtype, device=device)

def mul_out(x: torch.Tensor, y: torch.Tensor, *, out: torch.Tensor) -> None:
    torch.mul(torch.mul(x, y), x, out=out)

def fake_mul_out(x: torch.Tensor, y: torch.Tensor, *, out: torch.Tensor) -> None:
    return None

direct_register_custom_op(
    "mul",
    mul_out,
    mutates_args=["out"] if os.environ.get("ADD_MUTATES_ARGS", "0") == "1" else [],
    fake_impl=fake_mul_out,
    tags=(),
)


def mul_out_impl(x: torch.Tensor, y: torch.Tensor):
    out = create_output(x.shape, x.dtype, x.device)
    torch.ops.test.mul(x, y, out=out)
    return out


if __name__ == "__main__":
    torch.cuda.manual_seed(42)
    x = torch.randn(4096, device="cuda", dtype=torch.bfloat16)
    y = torch.randn(4096, device="cuda", dtype=torch.bfloat16)

    out1 = x * y * x
    s = torch.ones_like(x)
    del s
    out2_compiled = torch.compile(mul_out_impl)(x, y)
    print(out1.sum())
    print(out2_compiled.sum())
```

**Test script**
```bash
REMOVE_OLD_CACHE=1 python test_fx_graph_cache.py
ADD_MUTATES_ARGS=1 python test_fx_graph_cache.py
REMOVE_OLD_CACHE=1 ADD_MUTATES_ARGS=1 python test_fx_graph_cache.py
```

### Error logs

**Current**
```log
tensor(-138., device='cuda:0', dtype=torch.bfloat16)
tensor(4128., device='cuda:0', dtype=torch.bfloat16)
tensor(-138., device='cuda:0', dtype=torch.bfloat16)
tensor(4096., device='cuda:0', dtype=torch.bfloat16)
tensor(-138., device='cuda:0', dtype=torch.bfloat16)
tensor(-138., device='cuda:0', dtype=torch.bfloat16)
```

**Expected**
```log
tensor(-138., device='cuda:0', dtype=torch.bfloat16)
tensor(4128., device='cuda:0', dtype=torch.bfloat16)
tensor(-138., device='cuda:0', dtype=torch.bfloat16)
tensor(-138., device='cuda:0', dtype=torch.bfloat16)
tensor(-138., device='cuda:0', dtype=torch.bfloat16)
tensor(-138., device='cuda:0', dtype=torch.bfloat16)
```

title: [pr-review skill] Add device portability checks for shared accelerator code

labels.name: topic: not user facing
labels.description: topic category

body:
Stack from [ghstack](https://github.com/ezyang/ghstack/tree/0.15.0) (oldest at bottom):
* __->__ #187317

Motivation: Most PyTorch code is shared across accelerator backends (CUDA,XPU, ROCm, MPS), but community contributors typically only have CUDA hardware. As a result, CUDA-specific code regularly leaks into device-generic paths
(e.g. torch.cuda.* calls, hardcoded device="cuda", CUDA-only backend classes like CUDACodeCache, hardcoded "cuda" in expected-string test assertions). These slip through review and only break later on other accelerators such as XPU.

This PR adds a "Device Portability" subsection to the PR review checklist covering these common failure modes, with guidance to derive the device from inputs / use device-agnostic APIs, and an explicit carve-out that device-specific code behind a proper device guard is fine. A matching bullet is added to the Review Philosophy so reviewers consistently apply it.

title: Support save_on_cpu offloading under first-order torch.func transforms

labels.name: release notes: torch.func
labels.description: release notes category for torch.vmap or torch.func.* APIs

labels.name: ciflow/torchtitan
labels.description: Run TorchTitan integration tests

body:
## Issue

Fixes #122729 (also #146036, #141845)

## Summary

`torch.func` (grad/vjp/jacrev/vmap-over-them) disabled saved-tensor hooks for all
transforms via a 2022 blanket guard, so `save_on_cpu` raised under any of them.
This scopes the guard to higher-order only: a single first-order transform now
allows the hooks; nested diff (grad(grad), hessian, jvp(grad), ...) still raises.
Also makes `save_on_cpu`'s pinned buffer use `empty_like` so it carries the vmap
batch dim.

The 2022 attempts (#89166, #89159, #88976) stalled because they tried to support
higher-order too, which needs unwrapping/re-wrapping functorch tensors and
stashing autograd metadata by hand. We only enable the hooks for one first-order
layer (which round-trips correctly) and keep raising for nested diff, so none of
that is needed.

Follow-up: non-reentrant gradient checkpointing builds on this — JetBrains-Research/pytorch#3.

## Checklist

- [x] Passes lint (`spin fixlint`)
- [x] Added/updated tests
- [ ] Updated documentation (if applicable)
- [ ] Included benchmark results (for PRs impacting perf)

## BC-breaking?

No

title: [xpu][feat][3/4] inductor: route MX scaled_mm_v2 fallback through v2 aten kernel

labels.name: ciflow/rocm-mi300
labels.description: Trigger "default" config CI on ROCm MI300

labels.name: ciflow/torchtitan
labels.description: Run TorchTitan integration tests

body:
The Inductor lowering for `_scaled_mm_v2` (`tuned_scaled_mm_v2`) previously lowered every recipe to the v1 `aten._scaled_mm` kernel when falling back to the ATen GEMM. That is incorrect for recipes with no Triton template (the MX variants BlockWise1x32/BlockWise1x16 and multi-level scales): the v1 kernel expects a different scale_b orientation than v2, so the scales would be misinterpreted.

Add an `aten._scaled_mm_v2` ExternKernelChoice and route these fallback recipes through it directly, so the scales keep their native v2 convention. Single-level float32-scaled recipes continue to use the v1 `aten__fp8_mm` choice.

This affects all backends that hit the MX/multi-level fallback path, not just XPU.

### PR Stack:
#181726 [xpu][feat][1/4]Implement scaled_mm_v2 for MXFP8/MXFP4/NVFP4 on XPU
#181727 [xpu][feat][2/4]Implement scaled_mm_v1 for MXFP8/MXFP4/NVFP4 on XPU
#187315[xpu][feat][3/4] inductor: route MX scaled_mm_v2 fallback through v2 aten kernel
#181728 [xpu][feat][4/4]Enable MXFP8/MXFP4/NVFP4 tests for XPU

body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

## Issue Description
### Problem
The current PowerShell script responsible for compiling Caffe2 protocol buffers (.proto) lacks robust error handling and optimization configurations. Specifically:

It does not explicitly check if critical input files exist before attempting compilation, leading to cryptic failures mid-build.

It lacks explicit handling for tracking the exit codes of external binaries like protoc, meaning a compilation failure can go unnoticed by the main build pipeline.

The generated protobuf code is not optimized for size/performance natively via runtime configuration options.

Proposed Solution
Update the PowerShell build step to:

Add Validation: Implement explicit Test-Path checks for $unprocessed and the source caffe2.proto files prior to modification.

Optimize Build: Append option optimize_for = LITE_RUNTIME; to the .proto files during processing to generate lighter, faster C++ runtime code.

Enhance Error Tracking: Wrap execution in a try/catch block and explicitly inspect $LASTEXITCODE after running the $protoc compilation command to catch underlying execution failures instantly.

### Context / System Info
OS: Windows (PowerShell scripting environment)

Component: Build / Caffe2 / Proto compilation

body:
Stack from [ghstack](https://github.com/ezyang/ghstack/tree/0.15.0) (oldest at bottom):
* __->__ #187311

Inductor generated callables compute primal tensors only. When a compiled
callable is invoked with dual tensor inputs, the generated runtime bypasses
normal dispatcher JVP propagation and silently returns an output without a
forward AD tangent. Dynamo guards the active dual level, but that does not
prevent a compiled graph from being executed with tangent-bearing inputs.

Fix this by wrapping CompiledFxGraph callables with a runtime forward-AD check.
When boxed inputs carry active forward gradients, the wrapper executes the
original FX graph instead of the generated callable, preserving tangents through
normal eager dispatcher rules. Cache artifacts now preserve the original FX
graph and lazily deserialize it only when a forward-AD input reaches a cache-hit
path; normal cache hits do not load the graph. A cache-key bit prevents reuse of
older entries that were saved without this fallback graph.

The hessian example in the issue is a separate double-backward limitation of
AOTAutograd: direct double backward through a compiled function raises that this
is unsupported, and the issue's hessian repro also fails with cudagraphs. This
change targets the Inductor-specific forward AD tangent loss.

Fixes #187284
Generated by my agent

Benchmark Results:
- Command: python /tmp/bench_187284.py
- Workload: 20 repeated FX graph cache hits for a small CPU Inductor graph.
- Baseline clean worktree: median_ms 26.183, mean_ms 38.392, cache_bytes 408348
- Patched: median_ms 23.849, mean_ms 31.93, cache_bytes 408348
- Normal cache-hit check: GraphPickler.loads count remained 0 without forward AD inputs; forward-AD cache hit lazily loaded once and returned tangent tensor(1.2000).

Test Plan:
- python test/dynamo/test_higher_order_ops.py -k test_forward_ad_dual_input_inductor
- python test/dynamo/test_higher_order_ops.py FuncTorchHigherOrderOpTests.test_jvp_call_torch_compile_fn FuncTorchHigherOrderOpTests.test_forward_ad_dual_input_inductor
- python test/inductor/test_compiled_fx_graph_serialization.py -v
- TORCHINDUCTOR_FX_COMPILE_MODE=SERIALIZE python manual regular-cache forward-AD repro
- TORCHINDUCTOR_FX_COMPILE_MODE=SERIALIZE python manual bundled-autograd-cache forward-AD repro

title: Add maximize parameter to LBFGS optimizer

labels.name: topic: not user facing
labels.description: topic category

body:
Fixes #126642

LBFGS is the only standard PyTorch optimizer without `maximize` support. A prior attempt (PR #81098) was closed because it only negated the gradient in one code path, causing the line search to fail.

Unlike simpler optimizers (SGD, Adam) where negating the gradient is sufficient, LBFGS also requires negated loss values because its strong Wolfe line search uses both `f(x)` and `∇f(x)` — both must be consistent for the Armijo and curvature conditions to evaluate correctly on the transformed objective `-f(x)`.

**Implementation:**
- Negate gradients inside `_gather_flat_grad()` when `maximize=True`, covering all three call sites (initial eval, line search via `_directional_evaluate`, and fixed-step re-eval)
- Negate loss in `_directional_evaluate` and at each `closure()` eval point in `step()`, so the line search operates on `-f(x)`
- Return original `orig_loss` to the caller (not negated)

**Changes:**
- `torch/optim/lbfgs.py`: Add `maximize` keyword-only parameter, negate grad+loss at all eval points
- `torch/testing/_internal/common_optimizers.py`: Add `maximize` and `maximize_strong_wolfe` test inputs

**Why this works mathematically:** Maximizing `f(x)` is equivalent to minimizing `g(x) = -f(x)`. By providing `∇g = -∇f` and `g(x) = -f(x)` to the L-BFGS algorithm, the two-loop recursion, H_diag scaling, and Wolfe line search all operate correctly on `g` and converge to a maximum of `f`.

title: [MPS] Migrate argmin/argmax from MPSGraph to Metal kernels

labels.name: release notes: mps
labels.description: Release notes category

labels.name: ciflow/mps
labels.description: Run MPS tests (subset of trunk)

body:
Stack from [ghstack](https://github.com/ezyang/ghstack/tree/0.14.0) (oldest at bottom):
* __->__ #187304

Replaces the MPSGraph-based argmax/argmin implementation with three native Metal kernels mirroring the value_reduction layout introduced for amax/amin/all/any in #180752: a generic NormParams kernel for arbitrary reduction patterns, an inner-dim kernel (one simdgroup per row, simd_arg_reduce collapse) for contiguous reductions over the last dim, and an outer-dim kernel (TG_X x TG_Y tile, shared-mem pair tree reduce) for contiguous reductions over dim 0. The host-side dispatcher in ReduceOps.mm picks inner/outer when the input and output are contiguous and exactly one of those dims is reduced, and falls back to the generic kernel otherwise; full reduction (dim=None) is handled via a contiguous flatten so the returned linear index matches the standard as-if-contiguous convention.

The MPSGraph path used to cast bfloat16 to float32 before the reduction, so bf16 is where the migration wins the most (e.g. 4096x4096 dim=0 argmax goes 1518us -> 294us = 5.17x on M4 Max); fp16/fp32 dim=0 hit 2.4-3.5x and dim=1 hit 1.1-1.3x.

Correctness: the natural simd_argmax(val, idx) helper ties on lowest LANE, but when a single lane scans multiple positions its stored idx is not necessarily the lowest one carrying the winning value, so the kernels do a two-step simd_max + simd_min-on-eff_idx pattern instead (NaN lanes count as winners so first-NaN-in-source-order wins). The shared-memory tree reduction in the outer kernel uses the same strict-better-or-equal-with-lower-idx predicate. The NaN-propagating "should-replace" predicates argmax_replace / argmin_replace are factored into c10/metal/reduction_utils.h so they sit next to simd_argmax/simd_argmin, which already encode the same NaN-as-winner logic via simd_ballot.

TODO: argmin/argmax and amin/amax now share the (general, inner, outer) kernel shape, the per-thread-scan + simd-collapse skeleton, and the address-math helpers. Unifying them under a single Reducer concept (per-thread accumulator + finalize hook, with NCHAINS as a property of the reducer) would let the three kernel grids be written once and instantiated for both ValueReducer<Op, Load, NCHAINS=8> and ArgReducer<Op, NCHAINS=1>. Worth doing in a follow-up.

Benchmark (median of 500 iters on M4 Max, agent_space/bench_argminmax_pr.py):

| op     | shape     | dtype    | mode | baseline (us) | new (us) | speedup |
|--------|-----------|----------|------|--------------:|---------:|--------:|
| argmax | 4096x4096 | bfloat16 | dim0 |          1518 |      294 |  5.17x  |
| argmax | 4096x4096 | bfloat16 | dim1 |           991 |      315 |  3.15x  |
| argmax | 4096x4096 | float16  | dim0 |          1024 |      290 |  3.53x  |
| argmin | 4096x4096 | float16  | dim0 |          1014 |      326 |  3.11x  |
| argmax | 4096x4096 | float32  | dim0 |          1009 |      424 |  2.38x  |
| argmin | 4096x4096 | int64    | dim0 |          1075 |      740 |  1.45x  |
| argmax | 1024x1024 | bfloat16 | dim0 |           192 |      100 |  1.92x  |
| argmax | 1024x1024 | float32  | dim0 |           194 |      120 |  1.61x  |
| argmax | 4096x4096 | float32  | full |          5044 |     4926 |  1.02x  |

body:
### Problem description:
Running the timm benchmark with certain newer models (e.g. timm/gemma4_vit_570m_enc.gemma4_31b_it) fails at model load time with:
```
Gemma4VitEncoder.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'drop_rate'
```

### Reproduction
```
python \
    benchmarks/dynamo/timm_models.py --performance --inference --bfloat16 \
    --backend=inductor --output=timm_models_inference.csv --device xpu \
    --only timm/gemma4_vit_570m_enc.gemma4_31b_it
```
Or in isolation:
```
from timm.models import create_model
create_model(
    'timm/gemma4_vit_570m_enc.gemma4_31b_it', in_chans=3, scriptable=False,
    num_classes=None, drop_rate=0.0, drop_path_rate=None, drop_block_rate=None,
    pretrained=True,
)
```

### Root Cause
`_download_model` in `benchmarks/dynamo/timm_models.py` hardcodes `drop_rate`, `drop_path_rate`,
and `drop_block_rate` kwargs for all models. Newer timm model architectures (like Gemma4VitEncoder) do not accept these kwargs.

### Possible fix:
Retry `create_model` without the drop kwargs on TypeError:
```
@download_retry_decorator
def _download_model(self, model_name):
    try:
        model = create_model(
            model_name,
            in_chans=3, scriptable=False, num_classes=None,
            drop_rate=0.0, drop_path_rate=None, drop_block_rate=None,
            pretrained=True,
        )
    except TypeError:
        model = create_model(
            model_name,
            in_chans=3, scriptable=False, num_classes=None,
            pretrained=True,
        )
    return model
```

body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

"""
torch.nn.functional.unfold — RuntimeError instead of ValueError for zero stride

torch.nn.functional.unfold raises RuntimeError (from the im2col/unfold kernel)
when `stride` contains a zero element.  The rejection is correct, but the
exception type is RuntimeError rather than ValueError — inconsistent with
Python/numpy conventions for invalid argument values at the API boundary.

  RuntimeError: stride should be greater than zero, but got
    stride_height: 0 stride_width: 1

Failing example (fuzz crash, Specialized generator, cpu):
  shape=(1, 1, 13, 10)  dtype=bfloat16
  kernel_size=(1, 2)  dilation=(1, 1)  padding=(0, 1)  stride=(0, 1)
  hash [4a27cc61] × 87

Expected: ValueError at argument-validation time (stride must be > 0).
Actual:   RuntimeError from inside the unfold/im2col implementation.

Related:  fold/unfold share im2col paths; zero/negative stride, kernel, and
          dilation likely hit the same pattern.
Affected op:     torch.nn.functional.unfold (and likely torch.nn.functional.fold)
Affected device: cpu (same class of kernel-level RuntimeError on cuda/mps)
Not affected:    stride > 0 with otherwise valid 4D NCHW inputs
Versions:        PyTorch 2.12.0, macOS arm64
"""

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

SHAPE = (1, 1, 13, 10)
DTYPE = torch.bfloat16
KERNEL_SIZE = (1, 2)
DILATION = (1, 1)
PADDING = (0, 1)
STRIDE = (0, 1)


print(f"torch version: {torch.__version__}")

x = torch.randn(SHAPE, dtype=DTYPE)

# ── Baseline: valid kwargs should succeed ────────────────────────────────────
print("[baseline] valid unfold on CPU:")
out_ok = F.unfold(
    x,
    kernel_size=KERNEL_SIZE,
    dilation=DILATION,
    padding=PADDING,
    stride=(1, 1),
)
print(f"    input={SHAPE}  stride=(1,1)  ->  out={tuple(out_ok.shape)}")

# ── Fuzz repro: zero stride height ───────────────────────────────────────────
print("\n[bug] zero stride on CPU (fuzz example):")
print(f"    shape={SHAPE}  dtype={DTYPE}")
print(f"    k={KERNEL_SIZE}  d={DILATION}  p={PADDING}  s={STRIDE}")
print("    Expected: ValueError (stride must be > 0)")
try:
    out = F.unfold(
        x,
        kernel_size=KERNEL_SIZE,
        dilation=DILATION,
        padding=PADDING,
        stride=STRIDE,
    )
    print(f"    UNEXPECTED ok: {tuple(out.shape)}")
except RuntimeError as e:
    print(f"    RuntimeError: {e}")
except ValueError as e:
    print(f"    ValueError: {e}")

# ── Related invalid kwargs (same error class) ────────────────────────────────
print("\n[related] zero kernel size:")
try:
    F.unfold(x, kernel_size=(0, 2), dilation=DILATION, padding=PADDING, stride=(1, 1))
except Exception as e:
    print(f"    {type(e).__name__}: {e}")

print("\n[related] negative stride:")
try:
    F.unfold(x, kernel_size=KERNEL_SIZE, dilation=DILATION, padding=PADDING, stride=(1, -1))
except Exception as e:
    print(f"    {type(e).__name__}: {e}")

title: convert_sync_batchnorm silently drops the activation / extra behavior of `_BatchNorm` subclasses (e.g. timm `BatchNormAct2d`) - please emit a warning

labels.name: oncall: distributed
labels.description: Add this issue/PR to distributed oncall triage queue

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

labels.name: enhancement
labels.description: Not as big of a feature, but technically not a bug. Should be easy to fix

labels.name: module: ddp

labels.name: oncall: distributed parallelisms
labels.description: Add this issue/PR to the distributed parallelisms oncall triage queue

body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

`torch.nn.SyncBatchNorm.convert_sync_batchnorm()` rebuilds **every** `_BatchNorm` subclass as a *bare* `SyncBatchNorm`, copying only the BatchNorm attributes (running stats, weight, bias, eps, momentum). If the subclass added behavior - an overridden `forward`, a fused activation, dropout - that behavior is **silently discarded**. Child modules are even copied onto the new `SyncBatchNorm` (so `.act` is still *present*), but `SyncBatchNorm.forward` never calls them, so they become dead attributes. No error, no warning.

The result is a model that **computes a different function after conversion than before it**, with nothing to alert the user.

### Minimal, self-contained repro (pure PyTorch, CPU, no distributed init)

```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# A BatchNorm subclass that fuses an activation after normalization -
# exactly the shape of timm's BatchNormAct2d (pervasive in timm encoders).
class BatchNormAct2d(nn.BatchNorm2d):
    def __init__(self, num_features):
        super().__init__(num_features)
        self.act = nn.ReLU()
    def forward(self, x):
        return self.act(super().forward(x))   # normalize -> activation

torch.manual_seed(0)
model = nn.Sequential(BatchNormAct2d(4)).eval()
x = torch.randn(2, 4, 8, 8)
before = model(x)

converted = nn.SyncBatchNorm.convert_sync_batchnorm(model).eval()
after = converted(x)

before = before.detach()
after  = after.detach()

print("converted submodule type :", type(converted[0]).__name__)
print("ReLU applied before convert (min>=0):", float(before.min()) >= 0)
print("ReLU applied after  convert (min>=0):", float(after.min())  >= 0)
print("outputs differ after convert        :", not torch.allclose(before, after))
print("orphaned .act still present, unused  :", hasattr(converted[0], "act"))
print("max abs diff                         :", float((before - after).abs().max()))
```

Output (torch 2.11.0):

```
converted submodule type : SyncBatchNorm
ReLU applied before convert (min>=0): True
ReLU applied after  convert (min>=0): False  # <-- activation gone, negatives pass through
outputs differ after convert        : True
orphaned .act still present, unused : True  # <-- ReLU copied onto SyncBatchNorm but never called
max abs diff                        : 3.1537086963653564
```

The activation is dropped, yet `.act` is still attached to the module - it's just never invoked. Nothing warns.

This reproduces unchanged on the latest nightly (`2.14.0.dev20260614`), not just the release build below.

### Real-world impact

This is the single most common multi-GPU setup for any [timm](https://github.com/huggingface/pytorch-image-models)-based model. timm's encoders use `BatchNormAct2d` (fused BN + activation) *pervasively* - e.g. a MobileNetV4 encoder has **36 fused ReLUs**. The instant you wrap for DDP with `torch.nn.SyncBatchNorm.convert_sync_batchnorm`, all 36 activations vanish.

The failure mode is maximally nasty because **it is silent and deferred**:
- The model trains fine on the (silently degenerate) graph and reports a healthy validation metric.
- At inference / ONNX-export time the model is rebuilt from the *original* module definitions (activations present), the trained weights are loaded into a now-different graph, and the model collapses - in our case a segmentation model that logged **0.82 val F1 exported to 0.00** (all-background predictions).
- We burned ~2.6 days on 4 GPUs before realizing the trained graph and the exported graph were different networks. The clue was only visible by diffing module *types* after conversion (`{SyncBatchNorm}` vs `{BatchNorm2d, BatchNormAct2d}`).

A single warning at conversion time would have made this a five-minute fix instead of a multi-day investigation.

### Other libraries already work around this

- **timm** has had this reported since 2022 and ships its own fix - [huggingface/pytorch-image-models#1254 "[BUG] Broken SyncBatchNorm due to BatchNormAct2d"](https://github.com/huggingface/pytorch-image-models/issues/1254). Their EfficientNet repro went from a sane output to `-1.4e17` after conversion. Fix: a custom [`SyncBatchNormAct`](https://github.com/huggingface/pytorch-image-models/blob/fbe27d6c935a00e357e13f2f74043645769bd5fc/timm/layers/norm_act.py#L167) + [`timm.layers.convert_sync_batchnorm`](https://github.com/huggingface/pytorch-image-models/blob/main/timm/utils/model.py) that re-applies `.act`/`.drop`.
- **mmengine** ships its own [`convert_sync_batchnorm`](https://mmengine.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/generated/mmengine.model.convert_sync_batchnorm.html).
- **detectron2** ships its own conversion [helpers](https://github.com/facebookresearch/detectron2/blob/main/detectron2/layers/batch_norm.py) as well.

Three major libraries independently reimplemented this function specifically to route around the stock one. That's a strong signal the stock converter has a sharp edge worth guarding.

### Precedent: PyTorch already treated this `isinstance(module, _BatchNorm)` overreach as a bug

`convert_sync_batchnorm` selects layers with `isinstance(module, _BatchNorm)`. That check *used to* also catch `nn.InstanceNorm*`, because `_InstanceNorm` inherited from `_BatchNorm` - so instance-norm layers were silently converted to `SyncBatchNorm` and broken. PyTorch fixed this in commit [`37ca5a8`](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/commit/37ca5a8a644a21efcb1bf37c1da66ef81133af06) - *"convert_sync_batchnorm should not convert _InstanceNorm instances"* - by introducing a shared `_NormBase` base class and **reparenting `_InstanceNorm` out from under `_BatchNorm`**, so the `isinstance(module, _BatchNorm)` check no longer matches it.

So the project already recognizes this converter wrongly swallowing `_BatchNorm`-matching layers as a real defect. That fix worked because `_InstanceNorm` is in-tree and could be reparented. External subclasses like timm's `BatchNormAct2d` legitimately **are** `BatchNorm` layers (they genuinely want the synced BN) and cannot be reparented out - so the only general remedy is to **warn** when extra behavior is about to be silently dropped.

### Expected behavior / proposed fix

I'm **not** asking PyTorch to magically preserve arbitrary subclass behavior - that's not knowable in general. Just make the silent data-loss loud:

> When `convert_sync_batchnorm` is about to rebuild a `module` whose `type(module)` is a strict subclass of `_BatchNorm` that **overrides `forward`** and/or **has child modules / extra registered attributes**, emit a `warnings.warn(...)` (or `UserWarning`) saying that custom behavior will not be carried over to the bare `SyncBatchNorm` and may silently change the model's output.

This is cheap, non-breaking, and would have caught the timm/mmengine/detectron2 footgun at the source. A heuristic like `type(module).forward is not _BatchNorm.forward` or `len(list(module.children())) > 0` would cover the real cases.

Happy to send a PR for the warning if maintainers agree on the approach.

title: torch.where backward propagates NaN gradient from inactive sqrt branch

labels.name: module: autograd
labels.description: Related to torch.autograd, and the autograd engine in general

labels.name: bot-triaged
labels.description: This is a label only to be used by the auto triage bot

body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

`torch.autograd` produces `NaN` gradients for a `torch.where` expression when the inactive branch contains `torch.sqrt` evaluated on negative inputs.

In the example below, all input values are negative, so the selected branch is the identity branch `xt`. Therefore, the output is simply `xt.sum()`, and the gradient with respect to each input element should be `1.0`. However, PyTorch returns `nan` gradients, apparently due to the inactive `torch.sqrt(xt)` branch.

This issue reproduces on CPU, so CUDA availability does not affect the result.

```
import torch

xt = torch.tensor([-1.0, -4.0], dtype=torch.float32, requires_grad=True)

y = torch.where(xt > 0, torch.sqrt(xt), xt).sum()
y.backward()

actual = xt.grad
expected = torch.tensor([1.0, 1.0], dtype=torch.float32)

print("actual:")
print(actual)
print("expected:")
print(expected)
```

### Actual vs expected result

```text
actual:
tensor([nan, nan])

expected:
tensor([1., 1.])
```

The discrepancy occurs because the selected branch is `xt` for all elements, so the gradient should be finite and equal to `1.0`. Instead, `torch.autograd` returns `nan` gradients, seemingly propagated from the inactive `sqrt` branch.

body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

`torch.heaviside` returns `0.0` when the input is `NaN`.

For floating-point inputs, `NaN` values are expected to be propagated, consistent with NumPy's `np.heaviside`. However, PyTorch returns `0.0` for the `NaN` input.

This issue reproduces on CPU, so CUDA availability does not affect the result.

```
import numpy as np
import torch

x = np.array([np.nan, 0.0, -1.0], dtype=np.float32)
values = np.array([1.0, 1.0, 1.0], dtype=np.float32)

actual = torch.heaviside(torch.tensor(x), torch.tensor(values))
expected = torch.tensor(np.heaviside(x, values), dtype=torch.float32)

print("actual:")
print(actual)
print("expected:")
print(expected)
```

### Actual vs expected result

```text
actual:
tensor([0., 1., 0.])

expected:
tensor([nan, 1., 0.])
```

The discrepancy occurs for the `NaN` input. `torch.heaviside` returns `0.0`, while the expected behavior is to preserve `NaN`.

body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

torch.nn.functional.gelu with approximate="none" returns nan for a positive infinity input.

For exact GELU, gelu(x) = x * Phi(x). Since Phi(+inf) = 1, gelu(+inf) should be +inf. However, PyTorch returns nan for the +inf element.

This issue reproduces on CPU, so CUDA availability does not affect the result.

```
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

x = torch.tensor(
    [
        [float("nan"), float("inf"), float("-inf")],
        [-0.026216749101877213, 0.11177391558885574, -0.04212268441915512],
    ],
    dtype=torch.float32,
)

out = F.gelu(x, approximate="none")

expected = torch.tensor(
    [
        [float("nan"), float("inf"), float("nan")],
        [-0.01283420529216528, 0.06086074933409691, -0.02035370096564293],
    ],
    dtype=torch.float32,
)

print("out:")
print(out)
print("expected:")
print(expected)
print("allclose:", torch.allclose(out, expected, atol=1e-4, rtol=1e-3, equal_nan=True))
```

Actual vs expected result
actual:
tensor([[    nan,     nan,     nan],
        [-0.0128,  0.0609, -0.0204]])

expected:
tensor([[    nan,     inf,     nan],
        [-0.0128,  0.0609, -0.0204]])

The discrepancy is in the +inf input. For approximate="none", gelu(+inf) should return +inf, but PyTorch returns nan.

title: [Inductor][XPU] Skip torch.cond/while_loop in AOTInductorTestDualWrapper on XPU

labels.name: topic: not user facing
labels.description: topic category

labels.name: ciflow/torchtitan
labels.description: Run TorchTitan integration tests

body:
## Summary

gh#184736 wired `torch.cond`/`torch.while_loop` into AOTInductor dual-wrapper mode and removed XPU-specific skips from `GPU_LAZY_AUTOTUNE_TEST_FAILURES`. The CUDA path works, but on XPU the new codegen path crashes:

```
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'sycl::_V1::exception'
  what(): Backends mismatch
```

**Root cause:** Triton's `sycl_kernel_launch` (generated in `triton/backends/intel/driver.py`) calls `stream.submit(cgf)` with no surrounding try/catch. When the new cond/while_loop dual-wrapper codegen produces a kernel that triggers a SYCL `Backends mismatch` exception, it propagates out of the C extension — Python cannot handle C++ exceptions — and `std::terminate` is called, killing the entire pytest worker process. This caused failures across 6 CI shards.

**Fix:** Re-add XPU-only (not CUDA) `is_skip=True` entries to `GPU_LAZY_AUTOTUNE_TEST_FAILURES` for all `test_cond_*` and `test_while_loop_*` tests removed by gh#184736, plus `test_custom_op_in_subgraph` (which uses cond internally). `test_cond_symint_input_disable_one_pass` is already covered by `GPU_TEST_FAILURES`.

The underlying SYCL issue (why the new cond/while_loop codegen causes `Backends mismatch` on XPU but not CUDA) should be investigated as a follow-up.

Authored with an AI assistant.

## Test Plan

```bash

pytest test/inductor/test_aot_inductor.py \
    -k "AOTInductorTestDualWrapper" -v --timeout 600
```

3. Delete a deprecated Tensorboard test.

**more details for 2**
Most of the tests in this file are single-shot, no-warmup profiling tests, which makes them super susceptible to test pollution and other weird behavior. It looks like when we have a long wait/sleep duration where no CUDA events are happening after Kineto has been initialized, some events get filtered out during out-of-range checks. This issue goes away when you add a warmup to the polluted test.

This makes me think that there's something wrong with the timestamp checks in Kineto under this specific scenario that goes away when you allow a warmup -> active transition, but I haven't had time to debug it yet. For now, the simplest thing to do is to reduce the duration we are idle for.

title: nn: add torch.nn.functional.rotary_embedding and torch.nn.RotaryEmbedding
body:
Fixes #149534

## Summary

Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) is copy-pasted across many PyTorch-org repos (torchtune, torchao, gpt_fast, torchchat, benchmark, xla). This PR adds a canonical pure-Python/ATen implementation to core so those repos can adopt it and remove their local copies.

## What is added

**`torch.nn.functional.rotary_embedding(x, cos, sin, position_ids=None, *, interleaved=False)`**
- `x`: `(B, H, S, D)` — matches the layout of `scaled_dot_product_attention`
- `cos`/`sin`: shape `(N, D//2)` when `position_ids` provided; otherwise any shape broadcastable against `(B, H, S, D//2)` (e.g. `(S, D//2)`)
- `position_ids`: `(B, S)` int tensor that indexes into the cos/sin cache
- `interleaved=False` (default): half-half split — LLaMA / HuggingFace convention
- `interleaved=True`: adjacent-pair split — gpt-fast / torchtune convention

**`torch.nn.RotaryEmbedding(dim, max_seq_len=2048, base=10000)`**
- Precomputes cos/sin tables as non-persistent buffers (move with `.to(device)`, absent from `state_dict`)
- `build_rope_cache(max_seq_len)` is public and overridable by subclasses for custom frequency scaling (YaRN, NTK, LLaMA3-style)
- Raises `RuntimeError` with a clear message when `seq_len > max_seq_len`

## Implementation audit

Per the requirements from @mikaylagawarecki, all linked implementations were audited:

| Version | Distinct? | Layout | Interleaved | position\_ids | Complex | Notes |
|---------|-----------|--------|-------------|--------------|---------|-------|
| ONNX `torch/onnx/ops/_impl.py` | YES | 3D `(B,S,hidden)` or 4D `(B,H,S,D)` | Both via flag | Yes — 2D or 3D cache | No | Canonical reference; passes opcheck |
| gpt\_fast `benchmarks/gpt_fast/model.py` | YES | `(B,S,H,D)` | Yes — `reshape(-1,2)`, adjacent pairs | No — packed `freqs_cis` | No | Casts to float32 internally |
| torchtune base `torchtune/modules/position_embeddings.py` | YES | `(B,S,H,D)` | Yes — `reshape(-1,2)`, adjacent pairs | Yes via `input_pos` | No | nn.Module; builds its own cache |
| Llama3ScaledRoPE `torchtune/models/llama3_1/_position_embeddings.py` | YES | `(B,S,H,D)` | Yes | Yes | No | Adds YaRN-style frequency scaling |
| HuggingFace LLaMA | YES | `(B,H,S,D)` | No — `rotate_half`: `cat([-x2, x1])` | Yes — full-dim cache `(max_pos, D)` | No | See equivalence note below |
| Unsloth PR #238 | YES | `(B,H,S,D)` | Same as HF `rotate_half` | Yes | No | Triton kernel; excluded from scope |
| simple\_gpt / torchao / torchchat / mixtral\_moe | NO | Same as gpt\_fast | Same | Same | Same | Literal copies of gpt\_fast |

### Mathematical equivalence note

HuggingFace `rotate_half` is **mathematically equivalent** to the standard non-interleaved approach but uses a **different cos/sin cache format**:

- This API: `cos`/`sin` shape `(max_pos, D//2)` — stores half the angles
- HuggingFace: `cos`/`sin` shape `(max_pos, D)` — values repeated twice across last dim

The math works out identically (`rotate_half([x1,x2]) = [-x2,x1]` expands to the same rotation). The **incompatibility is in the cache format only**. Users migrating from HuggingFace must slice: `cos_hf[..., :D//2]`. This is documented in the docstring.

### Why no complex numbers

`view_as_complex` / `view_as_real` paths were considered and rejected due to known Inductor issues (pytorch/torchtitan#15). The pure real-number implementation compiles cleanly.

### Input layout choice: `(B, H, S, D)`

Chosen to match `scaled_dot_product_attention`. Users coming from torchtune (which uses `(B, S, H, D)`) must call `.transpose(1, 2)` on q/k before and after. This is documented in the docstring.

### torch.compile / `interleaved` flag

The `interleaved` bool drives a Python branch. When passed as a **literal** (`interleaved=True` / `interleaved=False`), torch.compile treats it as a compile-time constant → zero graph breaks. Passing a runtime variable causes recompilation on value change but does not error. Documented in the docstring.

### Frequency scaling

Standard frequencies (`theta_i = base^(-2i/dim)`) are in scope. YaRN, NTK, LLaMA3-style scaling are **out of scope** but supported via subclassing `RotaryEmbedding` and overriding `build_rope_cache`.

### XLA

Uses only standard ATen ops (chunk, mul, sub, add, cat, stack, unsqueeze) — all XLA-supported. Explicit XLA tests are a follow-up.

## Test plan

29 tests covering:
- Correctness vs. numpy reference (non-interleaved, interleaved, position\_ids mode)
- Mathematical equivalence between interleaved and non-interleaved paths
- position\_ids with arbitrary non-sequential positions
- Sequential mode identical to position\_ids=arange(S)
- Numerical accuracy of float16/bfloat16 vs float32
- Shape preservation across multiple input shapes
- dtype coverage: float32, float16, bfloat16
- Gradient check (`gradcheck`) for both interleaved modes
- `torch.compile` — zero graph breaks for both `interleaved=True` and `interleaved=False`
- `torch.export` — dynamic batch + seq dims, both sequential and position\_ids modes
- Module tests: correctness, device transfer, non-persistent buffers, `state_dict`
- `max_seq_len` overflow error + `build_rope_cache` rebuild + rebuild preserves device

```bash
python test/nn/test_rope.py   # 29 tests, all pass
```

title: `torch.compile` with `inductor` backend silently drops forward-mode AD tangents (returns `None`)

labels.name: module: correctness (silent)
labels.description: issue that returns an incorrect result silently

labels.name: bot-triaged
labels.description: This is a label only to be used by the auto triage bot

body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug


When a function is compiled with `torch.compile(backend="inductor")` and called inside a `torch.autograd.forward_ad.dual_level()` context, the forward-mode AD tangent (JVP) is silently dropped — `unpack_dual(...).tangent` returns `None` instead of the expected tangent tensor.

The same function produces the correct tangent in eager mode and with the `cudagraphs` backend.

## Minimal reproduction

```python
import torch
import torch.autograd.forward_ad as fwAD

def fn(x):
    return (x ** 2).sum()

compiled_fn_inductor = torch.compile(fn, backend="inductor")
compiled_fn_cudagraphs = torch.compile(fn, backend="cudagraphs")

x = torch.tensor([0.1, 0.2, 0.3])
v = torch.ones(3)

with fwAD.dual_level():
    dual_input = fwAD.make_dual(x, v)
    out = compiled_fn_inductor(dual_input)
    jvp_inductor = fwAD.unpack_dual(out).tangent

with fwAD.dual_level():
    dual_input = fwAD.make_dual(x, v)
    out = compiled_fn_cudagraphs(dual_input)
    jvp_cudagraphs = fwAD.unpack_dual(out).tangent

with fwAD.dual_level():
    dual_input = fwAD.make_dual(x, v)
    out = fn(dual_input)
    jvp_eager = fwAD.unpack_dual(out).tangent

print("inductor compiled jvp:", jvp_inductor)
print("cudagraphs compiled jvp:", jvp_cudagraphs)
print("eager jvp:", jvp_eager)
```

## Output

```
inductor compiled jvp: None
cudagraphs compiled jvp: tensor(1.2000)
eager jvp: tensor(1.2000)
```

title: torch.compile fails on Windows with `FileNotFoundError` (`.ttir`) when Inductor's descriptive Triton kernel name pushes the cache path past MAX_PATH (260)

labels.name: module: windows
labels.description: Windows support for PyTorch

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

labels.name: module: regression
labels.description: It used to work, and now it doesn't

labels.name: bot-triaged
labels.description: This is a label only to be used by the auto triage bot

body:
## 🐛 Describe the bug

On Windows (long paths **disabled**, the default), `torch.compile` aborts with a
`FileNotFoundError` for an Inductor-generated `.ttir` file whenever a fused
backward kernel gets a long *descriptive* name. Inductor names such kernels by
concatenating every fused op (e.g.
`triton_per_fused__fused_rms_norm__fused_rms_norm_backward_add_embedding_dense_backward_native_dropout_native_dropout_backward_1`).
The resulting full cache path exceeds the legacy 260-char `MAX_PATH`, so Triton
cannot reopen the `.ttir` it just wrote, and compilation dies.

A trivial pointwise kernel compiles fine, but kernels whose descriptive names
are long enough to cross 260 fail.

### Minimal repro

```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class M(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, vocab=128, dim=32, layers=2):
        super().__init__()
        self.emb = nn.Embedding(vocab, dim)
        self.drop = nn.Dropout(0.1)
        self.gains = nn.ParameterList(nn.Parameter(torch.ones(dim)) for _ in range(layers))

    def forward(self, x):
        h = self.drop(self.emb(x))
        for g in self.gains:
            h = h + F.rms_norm(h, (h.shape[-1],), g)
        return h

m = torch.compile(M().cuda())
x = torch.randint(0, 128, (8, 16), device="cuda")
m(x).sum().backward()
print("OK")
```

Reproduction depends on the *total* path length crossing 260, i.e. on
`len(inductor_cache_dir) + len(kernel_name)`. The snippet above fails even with a
short cache root (`E:\Temp\torchinductor_<user>`, path = **264**); on a default
install (`C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Local\Temp\torchinductor_<user>\...`, a ~140-char
prefix) much shorter kernel names already overflow.

### Error

```
torch._inductor.exc.InductorError: FileNotFoundError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory:
'E:\Temp\torchinductor\triton\0\QJtw5Dl1SLQpes2jQTHSICADfUXY5hnEN4WCy2ss5qI\
tmp.pid_10096_e9cd651e-f7f8-44b8-ab1e-9f34c73e0198\
triton_per_fused__fused_rms_norm__fused_rms_norm_backward_add_embedding_dense_backward_native_dropout_native_dropout_backward_1.ttir'
```

(raised from `torch/_inductor/runtime/triton_heuristics.py` during autotuning)

### Root cause

- Failing path length = **264** (and **283** for the original real-world model), both > `MAX_PATH` (260).
- `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\FileSystem\LongPathsEnabled = 0` (Windows default).
- Switching to short generic kernel names eliminates the long path and compiles fine.

### Workaround

In-process, before the first `torch.compile`:

```python
import torch._inductor.config as ic
ic.triton.descriptive_names = False   # short names -> path stays under MAX_PATH
```

Note: the env-var form `TORCHINDUCTOR_DESCRIPTIVE_NAMES=0` does **not** take
effect here, only the Python config flag works. Enabling Windows long-path
support (registry) would also avoid it but needs admin + a per-process opt-in.

### Suggested fix

Either side could own this:
- **Inductor**: cap/sanitize the descriptive kernel-name length on Windows (or
  whenever long paths are unavailable) so generated cache paths stay < `MAX_PATH`.
- **Triton (Windows)**: have the cache manager hash/truncate the on-disk
  filename, or open with the `\\?\` extended-length prefix, so a long kernel name
  can never produce an unopenable path.

## Versions

- PyTorch: **2.12.0+cu126** (also reproduces on **2.9.1+cu128**; worked on **2.6.0+cu124**)
- Triton: **triton-windows 3.2.0.post21**
- OS: Windows 11 Home (10.0.26200), long paths disabled
- Python: 3.12.10
- GPU: RTX 3060

title: Fix profiler_kineto.cpp build with USE_KINETO=0 and clean up clang-tidy

labels.name: ciflow/trunk
labels.description: Trigger trunk jobs on your pull request

labels.name: topic: not user facing
labels.description: topic category

body:
Stack from [ghstack](https://github.com/ezyang/ghstack/tree/0.15.0) (oldest at bottom):
* __->__ #187281

profiler_kineto.cpp calls fmt::format (in AddTensorboardFields) but never
includes a fmt header itself. It only compiled because fmt was pulled in
transitively through the libkineto headers, which are gated behind the
USE_KINETO macro. When building with USE_KINETO=0 that include block is
skipped, the transitive fmt/format.h disappears, and the file fails to
compile. The fix adds an explicit #include <fmt/format.h>, matching sibling
profiler files such as collection.cpp and util.cpp. fmt is always a libtorch
build dependency (third_party/fmt), so this is correct regardless of the
USE_KINETO value and removes the fragile reliance on a transitive include.

Modifying the file caused lintrunner to run clang-tidy over it (it only lints
changed files), surfacing pre-existing warnings that are treated as errors.
These are cleaned up so lint passes: std::set::count() membership checks are
replaced with contains() (count() is 0 or 1 on a set, so the behavior is
unchanged) per readability-container-contains; the two OpArgData aggregate
initializers now use designated initializers per
modernize-use-designated-initializers; and std::all_of(begin, end, ...) is
replaced with std::ranges::all_of(range, ...) per modernize-use-ranges.

Test Plan:
Lint passes clean on the file:

```
lintrunner torch/csrc/autograd/profiler_kineto.cpp
```

Authored with Claude.

title: MPS backend produces corrupted output in SDXL image generation on macOS 27 beta; CPU output is correct (primitive ops match CPU)
body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

After updating an Apple Silicon Mac to the macOS 27 ("Golden Gate") developer beta, PyTorch image generation (Stable Diffusion XL, via ComfyUI) on the MPS backend produces completely corrupted output — a uniform high‑frequency "noise / mush" image with no coherent structure. Running the identical workload on the CPU (--cpu) produces a correct, coherent image.

The regression appeared immediately on updating to the beta, with no change to PyTorch, the models, or the workflow. It worked correctly on macOS 26.

The corruption is independent of:


sampler / scheduler
prompt
attention backend (scaled_dot_product_attention path vs. split attention)
precision (default fp16/bf16 and forced fp32)


Only moving the computation to the CPU yields correct results.

What I was able to isolate

I compared isolated MPS ops against CPU reference outputs at half precision. scaled_dot_product_attention and conv2d, in both fp16 and bf16, match the CPU reference closely (no NaN/Inf):

fp16  attention relerr=0.0004  conv relerr=0.0004  nan/inf=False
bf16  attention relerr=0.0035  conv relerr=0.0029  nan/inf=False

(relerr = ‖mps − cpu‖ / ‖cpu‖, ref computed on CPU in fp32; tensors: attention q,k,v = randn(2,10,1024,64), conv x=randn(1,128,96,96), w=randn(128,128,3,3).)

So the fault does not appear to be in those primitives in isolation — it shows up only in the full model/sampler pipeline. I was not able to reduce it to a single-op minimal repro.

To Reproduce

Primary (what I observed):


Generate an SDXL text→image on MPS → corrupted noise.
Re-run the identical pipeline with the model on cpu → correct image.


Candidate minimal reproduction (⚠️ please run/confirm before relying on it — I could not fully verify which op triggers it):

pythonimport torch
from diffusers import StableDiffusionXLPipeline

model = "stabilityai/stable-diffusion-xl-base-1.0"
prompt = "a photograph of a dog in a park, detailed"

# MPS in fp16 (reproduces the corruption); CPU in fp32 as reference
for device, dtype in [("mps", torch.float16), ("cpu", torch.float32)]:
    pipe = StableDiffusionXLPipeline.from_pretrained(model, torch_dtype=dtype).to(device)
    img = pipe(prompt, num_inference_steps=20, height=512, width=512).images[0]
    img.save(f"out_{device}.png")
    print("saved", f"out_{device}.png")

Expected: out_mps.png ≈ out_cpu.png. Observed: out_mps.png is noise; out_cpu.png is correct.

Expected behavior

MPS output should match CPU output (coherent images), as on macOS 26.

Versions

Please paste the output of python -m torch.utils.collect_env here:

[run:  python -m torch.utils.collect_env  and paste the full output]

Known so far:


PyTorch: 2.10.0
Python: 3.11.14
OS: macOS 27.0 Developer Beta (build: ____ — from  → About This Mac)
Hardware: Mac mini (M4 Pro), 48 GB unified memory
torch.backends.mps.is_available() → True
App used to surface it: ComfyUI 0.21.1, SDXL checkpoint




### Versions

Additional context


Reproduces from a fresh process; not memory-pressure related (system memory pressure stayed green throughout).
Also filed with Apple via Feedback Assistant (Metal/MPS), since the root cause may be in the OS Metal framework rather than PyTorch.
Note: there is a separate, unrelated macOS 27 regression where host_statistics64(HOST_VM_INFO64) fails — that one is a macOS/psutil matter and has been reported to Apple; it is not a PyTorch bug and is mentioned only to avoid confusion if it appears in others' logs.

### 🐛 Describe the bug

A two-node `DistributedDataParallel` training job produces substantially different results when the same deterministic FP32 workload is executed with:
- CPU + Gloo
- CUDA + NCCL

Both arms use the same ResNet-18 architecture, initial seed, per-rank synthetic input tensors, labels, optimizer settings, world size, and number of optimize steps. The model is initialized from the same seed before being moved to the selected device.

After 20 AdamW steps, rank 1 reports:

- CPU/Gloo final loss: `2.5815484523773193`
- CUDA/NCCL final loss: `4.375443458557129`
- absolute difference: `1.7938950061798096`
- relative difference: `40.999%`

The final gradient norm also changes from `0.8940557241439819` on CPU to `2.136383533477783` on CUDA, a relative difference of approximately `58.15%`.

This is not an AMP, TF32, data-order, dataset-download, or cuBLAS nondeterminism comparison:

- precision is FP32 in both arms;
- AMP and GradScaler are disabled;
- TF32 is disabled;
- `torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(True, warn_only=True)` is enabled;
- cuDNN benchmark mode is disabled;
- `CUBLAS_WORKSPACE_CONFIG=:4096:8` is set before Python starts;
- inputs and labels are generated on CPU from fixed per-rank seeds and reused
  unchanged between the two arms;
- no dataset or DataLoader is involved.

Both distributed runs complete all 20 steps and exit normally. The problem is the reproducible numerical divergence, not a process crash.
## Reproduction

The following files are attached:

```text
minimal_repro_ddp_differential.py
pytorch_official_repro_config.json
```
You can get the repro code from https://gist.github.com/whitephilomel/92ca26f8b082b62c33ee5a5becb63ca2

The reproducer requires two machines with one process and one GPU per machine. Run it from the directory containing both files.

The relevant configuration is:

```text
model                     = resnet18
optimizer                 = AdamW
learning rate             = 1.0
weight decay              = 0.0
precision                 = fp32
AMP                       = disabled
TF32                      = disabled
gradient clipping         = 5.0
per-rank batch size       = 8
world size                = 2
steps                     = 20
seed                      = 0
DDP broadcast_buffers     = false
DDP bucket_cap_mb         = 128
DDP static_graph          = false
DDP gradient bucket view  = false
```

The complete configuration is contained in `pytorch_official_repro_config.json`.

## Actual behavior

The most recent confirmed run of `minimal_repro_ddp_differential.py`, performed with `CUBLAS_WORKSPACE_CONFIG=:4096:8`, produced:

| rank | CPU/Gloo loss | CUDA/NCCL loss | absolute difference | relative difference | CPU grad norm | CUDA grad norm |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 0 | 2.9489951133728027 | 4.01106071472168 | 1.062065601348877 | 26.478% | 0.8940557241439819 | 2.136383533477783 |
| 1 | 2.5815484523773193 | 4.375443458557129 | 1.7938950061798096 | 40.999% | 0.8940557241439819 | 2.136383533477783 |

Additional final-state differences:

| metric | CPU/Gloo | CUDA/NCCL | relative difference |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| gradient norm | 0.8940557241439819 | 2.136383533477783 | 58.151% |
| logits sum, rank 1 | 327.8002624511719 | 283.2908935546875 | 13.578% |
| parameter sum | -311665.84375 | -263532.53125 | 15.444% |

The decisive summary emitted on rank 1 is:

```json
{
  "status": "DIVERGENCE_REPRODUCED",
  "mode": "cpu_vs_gpu",
  "rank": 1,
  "reason": "arms diverged: final_loss",
  "final_loss": {
    "cpu": 2.5815484523773193,
    "gpu": 4.375443458557129,
    "abs_diff": 1.7938950061798096,
    "rel_diff": 0.409991586720532
  },
  "final_grad_norm": {
    "cpu": 0.8940557241439819,
    "gpu": 2.136383533477783,
    "abs_diff": 1.2423278093338013,
    "rel_diff": 0.5815097288788014
  }
}
```

## Expected behavior

Small floating-point differences between CPU and CUDA are expected, and bitwise equality is not required. However, with fixed inputs, fixed initialization, deterministic execution controls, FP32 computation, and only 20 optimizer steps, the two DDP arms should remain numerically close enough that the final loss and gradient norm do not diverge by approximately 41% and 58%, respectively.

If this degree of backend-dependent drift is expected for this supported configuration, it would be useful to document the expected numerical bounds and the operations responsible for the amplification.


### Versions

PyTorch: 2.9.1+cu128
Python: 3.11.15
CUDA reported by PyTorch: 12.8
NCCL: 2.27.5+cuda12.9
OS: Linux 5.15.0-113-generic x86_64, glibc 2.35

title: [XPU] torch.xpu.synchronize() SIGSEGV after XPU graph replay on Arc B-series (Battlemage) / oneAPI 2026.0 (torch>=2.13+xpu); stream-level sync is unaffected

labels.name: module: crash
labels.description: Problem manifests as a hard crash, as opposed to a RuntimeError

labels.name: module: regression
labels.description: It used to work, and now it doesn't

labels.name: module: xpu
labels.description: Intel XPU related issues

labels.name: bot-triaged
labels.description: This is a label only to be used by the auto triage bot

body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

On Intel Arc B-series (Battlemage), with the `+xpu` wheels that bundle the **oneAPI 2026.0** runtime (`intel-sycl-rt 2026.0.0`, i.e. `torch==2.13.0+xpu` and the current `2.14.0.dev*+xpu` nightlies), calling **`torch.xpu.synchronize()` (device-level sync) after replaying an XPU graph crashes with SIGSEGV** inside `urQueueRelease`.

The **graph capture and replay themselves are correct** — replaying produces the right results, and **stream-level synchronization (`torch.xpu.current_stream().synchronize()`) is completely fine**. Only the device-level `torch.xpu.synchronize()` crashes, and only after a graph has been captured.

This does **not** reproduce on `torch==2.11.0+xpu` / `2.12.1+xpu` (which bundle the oneAPI **2025.3** runtime), where device-level sync after replay works. It correlates with the bundled SYCL runtime version (2026.0), not the torch version per se.

#### Repro (pure `torch.xpu`, no other deps)
```python
import torch
x = torch.randn(512, 512, device="xpu")
out = torch.empty_like(x)

# warmup on a side stream (per the CUDA-graph protocol)
s = torch.xpu.Stream(); s.wait_stream(torch.xpu.current_stream())
with torch.xpu.stream(s):
    for _ in range(3):
        out.copy_(x * 2.0 + 1.0)
torch.xpu.current_stream().wait_stream(s)

# capture + replay a trivial graph
g = torch.xpu.XPUGraph()
with torch.xpu.graph(g):
    out.copy_(x * 2.0 + 1.0)
g.replay()

torch.xpu.synchronize()          # <-- SIGSEGV here (device-level sync)
# torch.xpu.current_stream().synchronize()   # <-- this variant is fine
print("done", float(out[0, 0]))
```

Result matrix (same machine, same script, only the post-replay sync call changed):

| post-replay sync | outcome |
|---|---|
| *(none)* | clean, replay result correct |
| `torch.xpu.current_stream().synchronize()` | clean, replay result correct |
| `torch.xpu.synchronize()` | **SIGSEGV** |

#### Backtrace (gdb)
```
Thread 1 "python3" received signal SIGSEGV.
#0  ur_loader::urQueueRelease(ur_queue_handle_t_*)        libur_loader.so.0
#1  ur_tracing_layer::urQueueRelease(...)                 libur_loader.so.0
#2  urQueueRelease ()                                      libur_loader.so.0
#3  sycl::_V1::detail::queue_impl::~queue_impl()           libsycl.so.9
#4  sycl::_V1::detail::device_impl::wait()                 libsycl.so.9
#5  c10::xpu::device_synchronize(signed char)             libc10_xpu.so
#6  THXPModule_xpuSynchronize(...)                         libtorch_python.so
   ... <Python: torch.xpu.synchronize()>
```
i.e. `device_synchronize` → `device_impl::wait()` ends up destroying a `queue_impl`, and `urQueueRelease` segfaults on a queue handle that appears to be already released / invalid after a graph capture.

#### Notes that may help localize
- **The SYCL/Level-Zero command-graph layer itself is healthy on this device.** A pure-SYCL `command_graph` (record → finalize → `ext_oneapi_graph(exec)` → replay ×2) compiled with icpx 2026.0 runs correctly on the same GPU and 2026.0 runtime. So the regression appears to be on the torch side (`c10::xpu` device-sync interacting with graph-capture queue lifecycle), not the SYCL graph runtime.
- Forcing the legacy adapter (`SYCL_UR_USE_LEVEL_ZERO_V2=0`) does not change it — still SIGSEGV.
- The crash is in **device** synchronize walking/releasing queues; **stream** synchronize does not touch that path and is safe.

#### Workaround
Use `torch.xpu.current_stream().synchronize()` instead of `torch.xpu.synchronize()` when a graph has been captured in the process.

*AI assistance was used to investigate and draft this report; the repro and backtrace were produced and verified on real hardware.*

### Versions

```
PyTorch version: 2.13.0+xpu
Is XPU available: True
XPU used to build PyTorch: 20260000
OS: Ubuntu 24.04 (x86_64), Python 3.12.3
Intel GPU driver: intel-opencl-icd 26.05.37020.3 / libze1 1.27.0
Intel GPU: Intel(R) Graphics [0xe223] (Battlemage), Level-Zero V2 adapter, driver 1.14.37020+3
bundled intel-sycl-rt: 2026.0.0
```
Also reproduced on `torch==2.14.0.dev20260614+xpu` (nightly, same bundled `intel-sycl-rt 2026.0.0`).
NOT reproduced on `torch==2.11.0+xpu` / `2.12.1+xpu` (oneAPI 2025.3 runtime).

title: [Dynamo] Trace raw unbacked SymInt inputs

labels.name: release notes: fx
labels.description: release notes category

labels.name: ciflow/torchtitan
labels.description: Run TorchTitan integration tests

body:
Stacked PRs:
 * __->__#187273
 * #183838
 * #187231


--- --- ---

### [Dynamo] Trace raw unbacked SymInt inputs


Non-strict tracing can pass raw unbacked SymInt values into a nested Dynamo trace. FlexAttention hits this when BlockMask.seq_lengths are derived from an unbacked query/key sequence dimension and then passed through block_mask.as_tuple() to the nested flex_attention_hop wrapper.

VariableBuilder previously only handled raw SymInt inputs when they had a guardable hint. Unhinted raw SymInt inputs graph-broke immediately, even if the nested graph only needed to carry them symbolically. This change keeps that support narrow: outside non-strict tracing, raw unbacked SymInt wrapping still graph-breaks. Inside non-strict tracing, Dynamo creates a ShapeEnv-owned unbacked SymInt input, reuses it for repeated occurrences of the same source expression, and records the transferred range/source/hint metadata through ShapeEnv events so recorded-event replay sees the same state.

The full non-strict FlexAttention repro also exposed a tracing-only issue: same-frame guard validation eagerly evaluates generated guards against fake inputs whose unbacked sizes cannot be concretized. The runtime guards are still produced, but the same-frame sanity check is skipped while inside the non-strict tracing context.

This keeps the important safety property unchanged: if user code branches on a raw unbacked SymInt, Dynamo still raises the normal data-dependent-symbol error instead of specializing on an optimization hint.

Fixes #187272

This PR was authored with assistance from an AI assistant.

title: Dynamo graph-breaks on raw unbacked SymInt inputs during non-strict FlexAttention tracing

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: This issue has been looked at a team member, and triaged and prioritized into an appropriate module

labels.name: bot-triaged
labels.description: This is a label only to be used by the auto triage bot

body:
## Issue

During non-strict tracing, FlexAttention can pass raw unbacked `SymInt` sequence lengths from `BlockMask.as_tuple()` into the nested Dynamo trace for `flex_attention_hop`. Dynamo's `VariableBuilder` currently rejects an unhinted raw `torch.SymInt` input with `Attempted to wrap unbacked SymInt`, even when the value is only used symbolically and no Python branch needs to be chosen from its concrete value.

This blocks tracing FlexAttention in flows that intentionally keep the token sequence dimension unbacked, for example graph chunking / non-strict tracing pipelines where the query/key/value token-grid sequence dimension is unbacked and the `BlockMask` sequence lengths are derived from that same symbolic dimension.

## Repro

```python
import torch
from torch.fx.experimental.symbolic_shapes import ShapeEnv
from torch._subclasses.fake_tensor import FakeTensorMode
from torch.nn.attention.flex_attention import BlockMask, flex_attention

shape_env = ShapeEnv()
seq = shape_env.create_unbacked_symint()

with FakeTensorMode(shape_env=shape_env, allow_non_fake_inputs=True):
    q = torch.empty((1, 1, seq, 8), device="cpu")
    k = torch.empty((1, 1, seq, 8), device="cpu")
    v = torch.empty((1, 1, seq, 8), device="cpu")
    kv_num_blocks = torch.ones((1, 1, 1), dtype=torch.int32)
    kv_indices = torch.zeros((1, 1, 1, 1), dtype=torch.int32)
    block_mask = BlockMask.from_kv_blocks(
        kv_num_blocks,
        kv_indices,
        BLOCK_SIZE=128,
        seq_lengths=(seq, seq),
    )

    with (
        torch.compiler._non_strict_tracing_context(),
        torch._dynamo.config.patch(force_compile_during_fx_trace=True),
    ):
        flex_attention(q, k, v, block_mask=block_mask)
```

## Actual

```text
torch._dynamo.exc.Unsupported: Attempted to wrap unbacked SymInt
  Higher Order Operator: torch.ops.higher_order.flex_attention
  Explanation: Unbacked SymInt input is not supported yet.

from user code:
   File "torch/nn/attention/flex_attention.py", line 2505, in _flex_attention_hop_wrapper
    return flex_attention_hop(*args, **kwargs)
```

## Expected

For branch-free symbolic uses, Dynamo should be able to represent the raw unbacked `SymInt` as a symbolic graph input in the nested trace. If user code actually branches on or concretizes that unbacked value, it should still fail with the normal data-dependent-symbol error rather than silently using an optimization hint as a guardable value.

## Root cause

`torch/_dynamo/variables/builder.py` handles raw `torch.SymInt` inputs by creating a dynamic symbolic input when `value.node.has_hint()` is true. When the raw `SymInt` is unbacked and has no guardable hint, it unconditionally graph-breaks instead of creating a Dynamo-owned unbacked symbolic input for branch-free tracing.

title: linear_cross_entropy: bound chunked peak memory with the budget chunking method

labels.name: module: nn
labels.description: Related to torch.nn

labels.name: release notes: nn
labels.description: release notes category

body:
Stack from [ghstack](https://github.com/ezyang/ghstack/tree/0.13.0) (oldest at bottom):
* #187053
* __->__ #187271

The chunked scalar op precomputes grad_input/grad_weight in forward, so at
its peak it holds both inputs and both gradients (2*(N+V)*in_features*
itemsize) plus the per-chunk working buffers. The aspect_ratio heuristic
sizes those buffers so the (chunk, num_classes) logits buffer matches the
input -- the efficient pick while num_classes >> in_features -- but it does
not size the (chunk, in_features) accumulator, which then grows as
chunk*in_features ~ num_batches*in_features^2/num_classes, quadratic in
in_features. So once in_features approaches num_classes the chunked peak
crosses the full-materialization reference.

This adds a 'budget' chunking method that sizes the chunk so the total
per-chunk buffer is about one input footprint, bounding it to a fixed
multiple of the input regardless of in_features; peak memory then stays
parallel to the reference instead of overtaking it. 'auto' keeps the
aspect_ratio pick below the crossing region and switches to budget above it
(in_features*acc_bytes >= num_classes*logits_bytes), so the common
num_classes >> in_features case is unchanged and only the large-in_features
regime gains the bound; an explicit aspect_ratio or budget is respected.

The default budget eps=1 ("buffer ~ one input footprint") is fixed by
continuity at the switch boundary, not by a memory objective: any
memory-only criterion is monotonic in eps and degenerates to eps=0 (one row
per chunk, slowest). eps=1 makes the budget chunk size meet aspect_ratio's
at the boundary, so the auto method change introduces no jump; 'budget:E'
overrides it.

The 2*(N+V) precompute floor is shared with the reference (and liger, which
precomputes likewise), so budget bounds the overage rather than undercutting
the reference -- the chunked path stays more accurate throughout (fp32-acc
logits vs the reference's input-dtype logits), which is why auto switches
method rather than falling back. Target-kind agnostic (index and prob).

This PR was authored with the assistance of an AI tool (Claude).

cc @albanD @mruberry @jbschlosser @walterddr @mikaylagawarecki

title: [serialization] fix torch.save creating/corrupting file when pickling fails

labels.name: topic: not user facing
labels.description: topic category

body:
`torch.save(obj, path)` currently opens the destination before serialization starts. If pickling fails in this case it can leave a new empty file or truncate/corrupt an existing file.

This PR makes path-based saves atomic by writing to a temporary file in the same directory and replacing the destination only after serialization succeeds. When it fails the temp file is removed and the original destination is left unchanged. File-like objects keep the existing behavior because arbitrary streams cannot generally be rolled back.

It also makes `_open_zipfile_writer_file.__exit__` exception-aware so `write_end_of_file()` is skipped after serialization errors and the original exception is preserved.

Fixes #48243.

## Test Plan

```
python test/test_serialization.py TestSaveAtomicWrite
```

Covers failed saves for new and existing paths, exception preservation, valid save after failure, `pathlib.Path`, legacy serialization, and file-like object behavior.

This PR was authored with the assistance of an AI coding assistant. I have reviewed the changes myself fully.. I take full responsibility for the code.

body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

Claude repro:
```
"""
Minimal repro: `torch._inductor.config.combo_kernels = True` crashes inductor
codegen with "Dynamic shape on reduction dimension is not supported" when a combo
kernel groups a *persistent* reduction whose reduction dimension is dynamic.

Run:
    python combo_kernels_dynamic_reduction_repro.py

Expected: prints "OK". Actual: raises during compilation:

    File ".../torch/_inductor/codegen/triton_combo_kernel.py", line 550,
        in codegen_static_numels_sub_kernel
      raise RuntimeError(
    torch._inductor.exc.InductorError: RuntimeError: Dynamic shape on reduction dimension is not supported

Three conditions are jointly required (drop any one and it compiles fine):
  1. combo_kernels = True. The scheduler groups >= 2 independent compatible kernels
     into one ComboKernel (no foreach op needed). Here the two `.sum()`s are grouped.
  2. A *persistent* reduction. inductor's heuristic
     (torch/_inductor/codegen/choices.py: should_use_persistent_reduction) picks
     persistent for a small contiguous (ReductionHint.INNER) reduction. For a dynamic
     numel this branch is only taken when the symbol has a static upper bound <= the
     threshold (64); the torch._check(...) calls supply it. Both reductions need a
     bound -- an unbounded one falls back to a looped (non-persistent) reduction and
     the group no longer trips the assert.
  3. The reduction numel is symbolic (dynamic=True). A persistent reduction emits
     RBLOCK == rnumel as a tl.constexpr, but ComboKernel.codegen_static_numels_sub_kernel
     requires every sub-kernel's reduction numel to be a static int and raises otherwise.

Workaround: torch._inductor.config.combo_kernels = False (the default) compiles fine.

Environment this was observed on:
  torch 2.12.0, torch.version.cuda 13.2, triton 3.7.0, NVIDIA B200 (sm_100), python 3.12
"""

import torch
import torch._inductor.config as inductor_config

inductor_config.combo_kernels = True
# Codegen happens at compile time; a stale on-disk artifact can otherwise mask the
# crash on reruns. Disable caching so the repro is deterministic.
inductor_config.force_disable_caches = True


@torch.compile(dynamic=True)
def fn(a, b):
    # Bound both reduction dims so they stay symbolic but inductor picks a
    # persistent reduction (statically_known_leq(numel, 64)).
    torch._check(a.shape[0] <= 32)
    torch._check(b.shape[0] <= 32)
    return a.sum(), b.sum()


def main():
    assert torch.cuda.is_available(), "needs a CUDA/Triton backend"
    a = torch.randn(16, device="cuda")
    b = torch.randn(16, device="cuda")
    print("OK", fn(a, b))


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```
